questions.question: What is a urinary catheter?
options: A dressing placed over a wound to keep it clean and dry.
A thin, flexible tube that drains urine from the bladder when a person cannot pass it in the usual way.
A monitor that measures a person's blood pressure.
A tube used to give someone food and fluids through the stomach.

questions.question: What usually holds an indwelling catheter in place inside the bladder?
options: Nothing; it is simply held in place by hand.
A stitch through the skin.
A small water-filled balloon.
Strong adhesive tape wrapped around the tube.

questions.question: Where does a suprapubic catheter enter the body?
options: Through a vein in the arm.
Through a small opening in the wall of the lower abdomen, directly into the bladder.
Through the back passage into the bowel.
Along the urethra.

questions.question: Where does a urethral catheter pass?
options: Into the stomach to drain fluids.
Through the abdomen directly into the bladder.
Along the urethra, the natural tube through which urine normally leaves the body.
Through a stoma in the side of the neck.

questions.question: Which staff may insert or remove a urinary catheter?
options: Only a registered nurse or doctor; never a care worker.
Anyone who has completed this awareness course.
Any care worker who has watched the task being done.
The person's family members.

questions.question: Under what circumstances may a care worker carry out delegated catheter care such as emptying a drainage bag?
options: Only after task-specific training, being assessed as competent, and the task being delegated by a registered nurse and set out in the care plan.
Only if no registered nurse is available at the time.
Whenever they feel confident enough to try.
As soon as they have finished this awareness course.

questions.question: What is meant by a closed drainage system?
options: The catheter, connecting tube and drainage bag forming one sealed circuit that keeps urine flowing away and germs out.
A system that is only ever opened at night.
A drainage bag fitted with a lid instead of a tap.
A catheter that is clamped shut and never drains.

questions.question: What is a CAUTI?
options: A device that measures a person's urine output.
A catheter-associated urinary tract infection.
A type of drainage bag worn only at night.
A fixing strap that secures the catheter to the leg.

questions.question: Why does having a catheter increase the risk of infection?
options: It does not; catheters lower the risk of infection.
Because it raises the person's blood pressure.
Because the tube gives germs a direct path into the bladder.
Because it makes the person drink less.

questions.question: What is the single most effective way to stop the spread of infection?
options: Emptying the drainage bag less often.
Clean hands, through good hand hygiene.
Applying antiseptic to the skin every day.
Wearing two pairs of gloves at once.

questions.question: What is asepsis?
options: Restricting the person's fluids.
Measuring urine output accurately.
Cleaning the floor around the person's bed.
Working in a way that keeps harmful germs away from a vulnerable site.

questions.question: How should urine normally be emptied from a drainage bag?
options: Through the outlet tap at the bottom of the bag.
By disconnecting the bag from the catheter.
By squeezing it out through the top of the bag.
By briefly removing the catheter.

questions.question: What is a night bag and how is it used?
options: A bag worn on the thigh during the day.
A larger bag attached to the outlet of the leg bag at night, keeping the day system closed.
A bag that permanently removes the need for a catheter valve.
A spare catheter kept for emergencies.

questions.question: Where must a catheter drainage bag always be positioned?
options: Above the level of the bladder.
At the same height as the bladder.
On the floor beside the bed.
Below the level of the bladder.

questions.question: Why must a drainage bag never be placed on the floor?
options: Because the bag drains faster when it is on the floor.
Because the outlet tap could freeze.
Because the floor is heavily contaminated and germs can travel up into the system.
Because it looks untidy in the room.

questions.question: When should a catheter drainage bag usually be emptied?
options: Before it becomes too full or heavy, generally around two-thirds full.
By disconnecting it whenever convenient.
Only when it is completely full to the top.
Only once a day, no matter how full it is.

questions.question: What should you do when recording a person's urine output?
options: Only record it if it looks abnormal.
Record the amount accurately and at the time, noting anything unusual about the urine.
Estimate the amount from memory at the end of the shift.
Round the figures up to make the chart look tidy.

questions.question: What does meatal hygiene mean?
options: Sterilising the catheter tubing.
Cleaning the drainage bag with antiseptic.
Keeping clean the area where the catheter enters the body.
Washing the person's hands before meals.

questions.question: What is normally needed for routine meatal hygiene?
options: An alcohol hand rub applied to the skin.
Mild soap and water.
Talcum powder and special cleansers.
A strong antiseptic solution.

questions.question: Why is it good to encourage someone with a catheter to drink well, unless they have a fluid restriction?
options: It means the drainage bag never needs emptying.
It removes the need for the catheter.
It makes no real difference but does no harm.
Good fluid intake keeps urine flowing, which helps flush the system and reduces blockages and infection.

questions.question: What is a catheter passport?
options: A legal document allowing a person to travel abroad with a catheter.
A personal record holding key information about the person's catheter that travels with them.
A permit that lets a care worker change catheters.
A waterproof cover fitted over the drainage bag.

questions.question: Which of these could be a sign of a catheter-associated urinary infection?
options: The drainage bag hanging below the level of the bladder.
A raised temperature, cloudy or foul-smelling urine, and new confusion in an older person.
Pale, clear urine and the person feeling completely well.
The person drinking plenty of fluids through the day.

questions.question: What is sepsis?
options: A mild irritation of the skin around the catheter.
The body's life-threatening reaction to an infection, which is a medical emergency.
A blockage in the drainage tube.
A normal effect of having a catheter in place.

questions.question: What should you do if a person's catheter falls out?
options: Flush it with water before replacing it.
Do nothing, as a catheter falling out is never a problem.
Reassure the person, note the time, keep them comfortable, and contact the registered nurse straight away.
Gently push it back in yourself to save time.

questions.question: What is bypassing in relation to a catheter?
options: When the night bag is attached to the leg bag.
When the catheter is changed by a registered nurse.
When the person drinks extra fluids.
When urine leaks around the outside of the catheter.

questions.question: Wearing gloves means you do not need to clean your hands before and after catheter care.
options: True
False

questions.question: A urinary catheter should only ever be inserted or removed by a registered nurse or doctor, never by a care worker.
options: True
False

questions.question: Routine meatal hygiene for a catheter usually needs only mild soap and water, not antiseptics or powders.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is safe to empty a drainage bag by disconnecting it from the catheter each time.
options: True
False

questions.question: A catheter drainage bag should always sit below the level of the bladder.
options: True
False

questions.question: The risk of infection falls the longer a catheter stays in place.
options: True
False

questions.question: New or increased confusion in an older person can be an early sign of a catheter-associated infection.
options: True
False

questions.question: If a catheter becomes blocked, a care worker should flush or unblock it themselves.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are correct infection-prevention practices during catheter care? (Select all that apply)
options: Clean your hands before and after the task
Rely on gloves instead of washing your hands
Wear clean disposable gloves and an apron for the task
Let the outlet tap touch the floor
Keep the drainage system closed

questions.question: Which of these could be signs of a catheter-associated urinary infection? (Select all that apply)
options: New or increased confusion in an older person
Cloudy, dark or strong-smelling urine
Pale, clear urine and the person feeling completely well
The drainage bag positioned below the bladder
A raised temperature or feeling hot and shivery

questions.question: Which of these are warning signs of sepsis? (Select all that apply)
options: Cold, pale or blotchy skin
Feeling completely well and comfortable
Fast breathing or a racing heart
Pale, clear urine

questions.question: For a care worker to carry out delegated catheter care, which of the following must be in place? (Select all that apply)
options: They feel confident enough to try without training
They have been assessed as competent
They have simply watched a colleague do it once
They have had task-specific training
The task has been delegated by a registered nurse and set out in the care plan

questions.question: Which of these should be reported to the registered nurse? (Select all that apply)
options: Urine bypassing, leaking around the catheter
Blood or clots in the urine
A drainage bag that is around half full
The bag correctly positioned below the bladder
No urine draining for a few hours

questions.question: Which of these are good practice when positioning the catheter and tubing? (Select all that apply)
options: Rest the bag on the floor when the person is in bed
Allow the person to sit or lie on the tubing
Keep the tubing free of kinks and loops
Keep the bag off the floor using a stand or holder
Keep the bag below the level of the bladder

questions.question: Which information may a catheter passport hold? (Select all that apply)
options: When the catheter was inserted and when it is next due to be changed
The person's bank details
The care worker's home address
The type and size of the catheter
Who to contact with problems

questions.question: Always keep the drainage bag ___ the level of the bladder.
An indwelling catheter is usually held in place inside the bladder by a small water-filled ___.
Urine should be emptied through the ___ at the bottom of the drainage bag, never by disconnecting it.
A ___ catheter enters through a small opening in the wall of the lower abdomen called a stoma.
A care worker must never ___ or remove a urinary catheter.
